paper_map.title: Effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low density δ-TRIP steel plate via intercritical quenching-annealing process
abstract_summary: The study examines the influence of annealing temperature from 600 to 800 °C on the microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel after intercritical quenching at 1050 °C.

The laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellae is retained, with varying austenite fraction, dislocation density and carbide precipitation depending on the annealing temperature.

Tensile tests reveal wide yield strength grades (460–620 MPa), excellent total elongation (40–63%) and low yield-to-tensile ratio (0.65–0.75), with the 740 °C annealed specimen achieving the maximum uniform elongation (~62.4%) and the highest product of strength and elongation (~45.2 GPa%).

Austenite stability governed by Mn and C partitioning leads to different levels of TRIP effect, and the work hardening behavior shows three stages with TWIP assistance, while the 680 °C sample exhibits early necking due to deficient TRIP effect.
paper_map.research_objective: To clarify how intercritical annealing temperature alters the laminated microstructure, austenite stability and TRIP/TWIP-assisted deformation behavior, thereby determining the achievable strength–ductility combination in a low-density Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C δ-TRIP steel.
material_systems: retained austenite (fine grains in tempered martensite)
main_process_variables: Intercritical quenching at 1050 °C for 1 h (water quench)
Annealing after quenching at 620, 680, 740, 800 °C for 1 h
